cases.title: THE MISSING COMMIT
cases.description: A critical commit has vanished from the repository. Track it down before it's too late.
cases.story_intro:
The rain pelted against my office window as the phone rang. Another case.

'Detective, we have a situation,' the voice trembled. 'Our repository... someone's tampered with the history. A crucial commit has disappeared.'

I grabbed my coat. Time to investigate the crime scene - a Git repository with secrets buried in its logs.

steps.instruction: First, let's check the commit history to see what's been going on.
steps.narrative: I walked into the dark server room. The humming machines cast eerie shadows. To find what's missing, I needed to see the full picture.
steps.hint: Use the command to view commit history

steps.instruction: Something looks wrong. Check the status of the working directory.
steps.narrative: The logs revealed a gap - commits that should exist were nowhere to be seen. I needed to check the current state of affairs.
steps.hint: Check the current state of your repository

steps.instruction: Let's see what changes were made but not yet staged.
steps.narrative: Clues were scattered everywhere. Unstaged changes might hold the key to this mystery.
steps.hint: Show changes between commits, commit and working tree

steps.instruction: Stage all the evidence - add all changes to the staging area.
steps.narrative: I gathered all the evidence carefully. Every piece matters in solving this case.

steps.instruction: Seal the evidence with a commit. The message should be 'Case solved'
steps.narrative: With all evidence collected, it was time to close this chapter. The missing commit would be replaced with our findings.
steps.hint: Commit your staged changes with a message

cases.title: THE BRANCHING CONSPIRACY
cases.description: Multiple branches lead to different suspects. Navigate through them to find the truth.
cases.story_intro:
The case file landed on my desk with a thud. 'The Branching Conspiracy' - a web of deceit spreading across multiple timelines.

'There are at least three branches of investigation,' my contact whispered. 'Each one leads to a different suspect. But only one holds the truth.'

I lit a cigarette. In this business, you learn to follow every branch until you find the root of the problem.

steps.instruction: List all the branches in this investigation.
steps.narrative: First rule of detective work - know all your leads. I needed to see every branch of this tangled case.
steps.hint: List all branches in the repository

steps.instruction: Create a new branch called 'investigation' to work on.
steps.narrative: I couldn't contaminate the main evidence. A separate line of investigation was needed.
steps.hint: Create a new branch for your investigation

steps.instruction: Switch to the 'investigation' branch.
steps.narrative: Time to dive into the shadows. I stepped into the new branch, leaving the main timeline behind.

steps.instruction: Check which branch you're currently on.
steps.narrative: In this labyrinth of branches, I needed to know exactly where I stood.

steps.instruction: The investigation is complete. Delete the branch 'old-suspect' that led nowhere.
steps.narrative: Dead ends happen. The 'old-suspect' branch was a false lead. Time to clean up.
steps.hint: Delete the branch that's no longer needed

cases.title: THE MERGE MURDER
cases.description: Two parallel investigations must become one. But merging them reveals a deadly conflict.
cases.story_intro:
The phone call came at midnight. 'Detective, we have a merge conflict. And this time... it's fatal.'

Two teams had been investigating the same case from different angles. Now their findings needed to come together. But the merge revealed inconsistencies - someone was lying.

I grabbed my fedora. Resolving conflicts is messy business, but someone's gotta do it.

steps.instruction: First, make sure you're on the main branch.
steps.narrative: Before any merge, I needed solid ground. The main branch was my anchor.

steps.instruction: Merge the 'evidence' branch into main.
steps.narrative: It was time to combine the evidence. Two stories would become one - or expose a liar.

steps.instruction: Check the status to see the conflict details.
steps.narrative: The merge failed. Conflicting testimonies. I needed to see exactly where the stories didn't match.

steps.instruction: After resolving conflicts manually, stage the resolved files.
steps.narrative: I pieced together the truth from both versions. Now to mark the conflict as resolved.

steps.instruction: Complete the merge with a commit.
steps.narrative: The truth was finally clear. One story, resolved. Case closed.

cases.title: THE REMOTE SYNDICATE
cases.description: A criminal network operates across remote servers. Connect and gather intelligence.
cases.story_intro:
'They call themselves The Remote Syndicate,' the informant said, glancing nervously around the dimly lit bar.

'They operate across multiple servers - origin, upstream, backup. Push and pull information like ghosts in the network.'

I adjusted my hat. To catch a network, you need to think like a network. Time to go remote.

steps.instruction: List all remote connections in this repository.
steps.narrative: First, I needed to map the network. Every remote connection was a potential lead.

steps.instruction: Fetch the latest intel from the origin remote.
steps.narrative: Information flows constantly in this network. Time to intercept the latest transmissions.
steps.hint: Download objects and refs from the remote

steps.instruction: Pull the latest changes from origin main.
steps.narrative: I needed everything they had. A full download of their latest operations.

steps.instruction: Push your investigation findings to the remote.
steps.narrative: Time to share my findings with headquarters. The evidence needed to be backed up.
steps.hint: Upload your local commits to the remote

steps.instruction: Add a new remote called 'backup' with URL 'https://backup.agency.git'
steps.narrative: Never keep all your eggs in one basket. A backup remote for redundancy.

cases.title: THE REBASE REDEMPTION
cases.description: History has been rewritten. Use advanced techniques to uncover the original timeline.
cases.story_intro:
They said the case was closed. They said the history was clean. But I knew better.

Someone had rewritten the timeline - a rebase so clean it almost erased the truth. Almost.

'Detective, this goes deep,' my partner warned. 'Rebasing history is dangerous territory.'

I smiled grimly. Danger is my middle name. Time to redeem the past.

steps.instruction: View the complete history including all refs.
steps.narrative: The official history was sanitized, but ghosts remain. I needed to see everything.
steps.hint: Show reference logs or all commits

steps.instruction: Start an interactive rebase of the last 3 commits.
steps.narrative: Time to dive into the timeline and examine each commit closely.
steps.hint: Start interactive rebase for last 3 commits

steps.instruction: Stash your current changes before the operation.
steps.narrative: Before going deeper, I needed to secure my current findings.

steps.instruction: Apply the stashed changes back.
steps.narrative: Operation complete. Time to restore my secured evidence.

steps.instruction: Reset the last commit but keep the changes staged.
steps.narrative: The final piece. Undo the cover-up while preserving the evidence.
steps.hint: Reset to previous commit, keep changes staged

cases.title: THE CLONE CONSPIRACY
cases.description: A mysterious repository holds the key. Your first mission: obtain a copy without alerting anyone.
cases.story_intro:
The informant's message was cryptic: 'The evidence is in repository X. Get it before they delete it.'

I needed to make an exact copy of that repository - every file, every commit, every secret. But I had to be careful. One wrong move and they'd know I was onto them.

Time to clone the evidence.

steps.instruction: Clone the repository from 'https://github.com/agency/evidence.git'
steps.narrative: The repository URL glowed on my screen. One command to copy everything.
steps.hint: Create a copy of a remote repository

steps.instruction: Navigate into the cloned repository directory called 'evidence'.
steps.narrative: The download completed silently. Now to enter the cloned directory.
steps.hint: Change directory into the cloned folder

steps.instruction: Check that the remote origin is properly configured.
steps.narrative: I needed to verify my connection to the source was intact.

steps.instruction: List all files to see what evidence we have.
steps.narrative: Time to see what secrets this repository contained.

steps.instruction: View the commit history to understand the timeline.
steps.narrative: Every commit tells a story. I needed to read them all.

cases.title: THE STAGED DECEPTION
cases.description: Learn to selectively stage evidence. Not everything belongs in the case file.
cases.story_intro:
'We found multiple pieces of evidence,' the junior detective reported, 'but some of it might be planted.'

In detective work, you don't submit everything to the court. You carefully select what's relevant. Same with Git - staging is an art.

I would teach them the power of selective staging.

steps.instruction: Check what files have been modified.
steps.narrative: First, survey all the evidence on the table.

steps.instruction: Stage only the file called 'witness.txt'.
steps.narrative: The witness testimony was solid. That goes in.
steps.hint: Add a specific file to staging

steps.instruction: Verify that only witness.txt is staged.
steps.narrative: Double-check. In this business, mistakes cost lives.

steps.instruction: Unstage witness.txt - we need to review it again.
steps.narrative: Wait. Something didn't add up. Better pull it back for review.
steps.hint: Remove a file from staging area

steps.instruction: Now stage all .txt files at once.
steps.narrative: After review, all text evidence was verified. Stage them all.
steps.hint: Use a pattern to stage multiple files

cases.title: THE IDENTITY CRISIS
cases.description: Before any investigation, a detective must establish their identity. Configure Git properly.
cases.story_intro:
The chief slammed the file on my desk. 'Your reports are coming in anonymous! How am I supposed to know who's solving these cases?'

Every commit needs an author. Every detective needs a badge. Time to configure my identity in the system.

No more anonymous work.

steps.instruction: Set your Git username to 'Detective Noir'.
steps.narrative: First, establish who I am in this system.

steps.instruction: Set your Git email to 'noir@detective.agency'.
steps.narrative: Every badge needs a contact. My agency email would do.

steps.instruction: Verify your configuration by listing all settings.
steps.narrative: Trust but verify. Let's see if the identity stuck.

steps.instruction: Check just the username configuration.
steps.narrative: Focus on the name - that's what shows on every case file.

cases.title: THE DELETED EVIDENCE
cases.description: Critical files were removed. Learn to restore what was lost.
cases.story_intro:
The witness panicked. 'I deleted the evidence by accident! It's gone forever!'

I smiled. In Git, nothing is ever truly gone. The history remembers everything. Time to restore what they thought was lost.

'Relax,' I said. 'I know a few tricks.'

steps.instruction: Check the status to see what files are deleted.
steps.narrative: First, assess the damage. What exactly was removed?

steps.instruction: Restore the deleted file 'evidence.txt' from the last commit.
steps.narrative: The file exists in history. We just need to pull it back.
steps.hint: Restore a file from the last commit

steps.instruction: Discard all local changes and restore the working directory.
steps.narrative: Too much was tampered with. Reset everything to the last known good state.
steps.hint: Restore all files to last commit state

steps.instruction: View what a file looked like 2 commits ago using show.
steps.narrative: I needed to see the file as it was before the tampering began.
steps.hint: Show file content from a specific commit

cases.title: THE TAG TEAM TAKEDOWN
cases.description: Mark important milestones in the investigation. Tags are permanent markers in history.
cases.story_intro:
The case was sprawling across months of commits. 'How do we mark the key moments?' my partner asked.

'Tags,' I replied. 'Permanent markers that never move. When we crack a major lead, we tag it. Future detectives will thank us.'

Time to mark our territory in history.

steps.instruction: Create a lightweight tag called 'arrest-v1' on the current commit.
steps.narrative: The first arrest was made. Mark this moment.

steps.instruction: Create an annotated tag 'case-closed-v1' with message 'First case closure'.
steps.narrative: For major milestones, annotated tags carry more weight - they have messages.
steps.hint: Create an annotated tag with a message

steps.instruction: List all tags in the repository.
steps.narrative: Let's see all our marked milestones.

steps.instruction: Show details of the 'case-closed-v1' tag.
steps.narrative: Examine the annotated tag - who created it and when.

steps.instruction: Delete the tag 'arrest-v1' - wrong suspect.
steps.narrative: We tagged the wrong moment. Remove that marker.

cases.title: THE CHERRY PICKER
cases.description: Extract specific commits from one branch to another. Surgical precision required.
cases.story_intro:
Two separate investigations had been running in parallel. One team found crucial evidence, but their branch was a mess.

'I only need that ONE commit,' I muttered. 'Not the whole chaotic branch.'

Cherry-pick. Take exactly what you need, leave the rest behind.

steps.instruction: View the log of the 'chaotic-investigation' branch to find the good commit.
steps.narrative: First, identify exactly which commit contains the evidence I need.

steps.instruction: Make sure you're on the main branch.
steps.narrative: Cherry-picking brings commits TO your current branch. Get positioned.

steps.instruction: Cherry-pick the commit with hash 'abc1234'.
steps.narrative: Extract that single commit. Surgical precision.
steps.hint: Apply a specific commit to current branch

steps.instruction: If there's a conflict, abort the cherry-pick operation.
steps.narrative: Something went wrong. Abort and reassess.

steps.instruction: View the log to confirm the commit was applied.
steps.narrative: Verify the evidence is now part of our clean investigation.

cases.title: THE BLAME GAME
cases.description: Track down who wrote each line of code. Someone introduced a bug - find them.
cases.story_intro:
The system crashed. Millions lost. 'WHO DID THIS?' the chief demanded.

Somewhere in that file, someone wrote a bad line of code. Git remembers everything - every line, every author, every timestamp.

Time to play the blame game.

steps.instruction: Use git blame on 'suspect-file.js' to see who wrote each line.
steps.narrative: Every line has an author. Let's see who wrote what.
steps.hint: Show who last modified each line

steps.instruction: Show blame for only lines 10 to 20 of the file.
steps.narrative: The bug is in that section. Focus the investigation.

steps.instruction: Use git log to see all commits that touched 'suspect-file.js'.
steps.narrative: Who has been modifying this file? Show me everyone.
steps.hint: Show commits affecting a specific file

steps.instruction: Show what changed in the file across the last 5 commits.
steps.narrative: Trace the recent modifications. The bug is recent.
steps.hint: Show patches for recent commits on file

cases.title: THE REVERT OPERATION
cases.description: A bad commit went to production. Undo the damage without erasing history.
cases.story_intro:
'The deployment broke everything!' The panicked call came at 3 AM.

Someone pushed bad code. We couldn't just delete the commit - that would destroy the audit trail. We needed to REVERT - create a new commit that undoes the damage.

Clean, traceable, professional.

steps.instruction: View the recent commits to find the bad one.
steps.narrative: First, identify the criminal commit.

steps.instruction: Revert the commit with hash 'bad1234'.
steps.narrative: Create a new commit that undoes the bad one. History preserved.
steps.hint: Create a commit that undoes another commit

steps.instruction: Revert the last commit using HEAD.
steps.narrative: The most recent commit was the problem. Undo it.

steps.instruction: Revert a commit but don't auto-commit - just stage the changes.
steps.narrative: I want to review the revert before committing.

cases.title: THE COMPARISON FILES
cases.description: Compare different versions of evidence. Spot the differences between branches and commits.
cases.story_intro:
Two versions of the same document. 'They look identical,' the rookie said.

I shook my head. 'Nothing is ever identical. There are always differences. You just need to know how to look.'

Git diff - the detective's magnifying glass.

steps.instruction: Show differences between the working directory and staging area.
steps.narrative: What changed since we last staged?

steps.instruction: Show differences between staging area and last commit.
steps.narrative: What's staged but not yet committed?

steps.instruction: Compare the current branch with the 'suspect' branch.
steps.narrative: How does our investigation differ from theirs?

steps.instruction: Show differences for only the file 'clue.txt' between two commits.
steps.narrative: Focus on one piece of evidence across versions.

cases.title: THE BISECT MANHUNT
cases.description: A bug was introduced somewhere in 100 commits. Use binary search to find it fast.
cases.story_intro:
'The bug appeared sometime in the last 100 commits,' the developer said hopelessly. 'It could take days to find.'

I smiled. 'Or minutes. Git bisect uses binary search. We'll find your culprit in about 7 steps.'

The most efficient manhunt in version control.

steps.instruction: Start the bisect process.
steps.narrative: Initialize the binary search operation.

steps.instruction: Mark the current commit as bad (it has the bug).
steps.narrative: We know the bug exists now. Mark this as bad.

steps.instruction: Mark commit 'abc1234' as good (before the bug existed).
steps.narrative: We know this old commit was working. Mark it good.

steps.instruction: After testing, mark the current commit as good.
steps.narrative: This version works. The bug must be later.

steps.instruction: Reset and end the bisect session.
steps.narrative: Investigation complete. Return to normal operations.

cases.title: THE SUBMODULE SYNDICATE
cases.description: The evidence spans multiple repositories. Navigate the web of submodules.
cases.story_intro:
The criminal organization was clever. They split their operations across multiple repositories, linked together as submodules.

'It's like a hydra,' my partner said. 'Cut one head, two more appear.'

Time to master the art of submodules - repositories within repositories.

steps.instruction: Add a submodule from 'https://github.com/syndicate/tools.git' into 'tools' folder.
steps.narrative: Link their tools repository into our investigation.
steps.hint: Add a repository as a submodule

steps.instruction: Initialize all submodules in the repository.
steps.narrative: Activate all the linked repositories.

steps.instruction: Update all submodules to their latest commits.
steps.narrative: Pull the latest evidence from all linked repos.

steps.instruction: Clone a repository and all its submodules in one command.
steps.narrative: Get everything at once - the parent and all children.

steps.instruction: Check the status of all submodules.
steps.narrative: Survey all linked repositories at once.

cases.title: THE WORKTREE PARALLEL
cases.description: Work on multiple branches simultaneously without switching. Advanced multitasking.
cases.story_intro:
Three cases demanded my attention simultaneously. Switching branches constantly was killing my productivity.

Then I discovered worktrees - multiple working directories, each on a different branch, all from one repository.

Time to work in parallel dimensions.

steps.instruction: Create a new worktree for the 'urgent-case' branch in '../urgent' folder.
steps.narrative: Create a parallel workspace for the urgent investigation.
steps.hint: Add a new worktree for a branch

steps.instruction: List all worktrees associated with this repository.
steps.narrative: See all my parallel workspaces.

steps.instruction: Create a new worktree with a new branch 'cold-case' in '../cold' folder.
steps.narrative: Start a new investigation in its own space.

steps.instruction: Remove the worktree at '../urgent' after the case is closed.
steps.narrative: Clean up. The urgent case is solved.

steps.instruction: Prune stale worktree information.
steps.narrative: Clean up references to deleted worktrees.

cases.title: THE REFLOG RESURRECTION
cases.description: They thought they erased everything. But reflog remembers. Recover the impossible.
cases.story_intro:
'It's gone,' they said. 'We reset --hard and force pushed. The evidence is destroyed.'

I laughed. They didn't know about reflog - Git's secret diary that remembers EVERYTHING. Every checkout, every reset, every move.

Nothing is ever truly lost.

steps.instruction: View the reflog to see all recent HEAD movements.
steps.narrative: The reflog sees all. Every action leaves a trace.

steps.instruction: Find all actions from the last 2 hours.
steps.narrative: Narrow down the timeline of their destruction.

steps.instruction: Restore to the state from reflog entry HEAD@{3}.
steps.narrative: Go back to exactly that moment in time.

steps.instruction: Create a new branch from a 'lost' commit using reflog.
steps.narrative: Resurrect a deleted branch by finding its last commit.

steps.instruction: View reflog for a specific branch called 'feature'.
steps.narrative: See the history of just that branch.

cases.title: THE CLEAN SWEEP
cases.description: The repository is cluttered with untracked files. Time for a deep clean.
cases.story_intro:
The crime scene was contaminated. Hundreds of untracked files - logs, temp files, build artifacts - cluttered everything.

'We need a clean sweep,' I said. 'Remove everything that doesn't belong. Git clean is our broom.'

Time to take out the trash.

steps.instruction: Show what untracked files would be removed (dry run).
steps.narrative: First, see what would be swept away without actually doing it.

steps.instruction: Remove all untracked files forcefully.
steps.narrative: Execute the clean sweep. Remove all untracked files.

steps.instruction: Remove untracked files AND directories.
steps.narrative: Go deeper. Remove untracked directories too.

steps.instruction: Remove untracked files including those ignored by .gitignore.
steps.narrative: The nuclear option. Remove EVERYTHING untracked.

cases.title: THE ARCHIVE EXTRACTION
cases.description: Export the repository without Git history. Create clean archives for evidence handoff.
cases.story_intro:
The prosecution needed the code, but not the Git history. 'Give us the files, not the secrets in the commits.'

Git archive creates clean exports - just the files, no .git folder, no history. Perfect for evidence handoff.

Time to package and deliver.

steps.instruction: Create a tar archive of the current HEAD.
steps.narrative: Package the current state into a tar file.

steps.instruction: Create a zip archive of the 'main' branch.
steps.narrative: Package for Windows users - they prefer zip.
steps.hint: Create zip archive of a branch

steps.instruction: Create an archive of only the 'src' directory.
steps.narrative: They only need the source code, not everything.

steps.instruction: Create an archive with a prefix directory 'evidence/'.
steps.narrative: Add a wrapper folder to the archive contents.
